summary: DHH examines an ILSR report revealing that Amazon's average cut from independent retailers has risen from 19% to 30% in five years, mirroring Apple and Google's marketplace fees. He argues this is a universal big tech monopoly pattern: once a company controls a tollbooth that businesses must pass through, it inevitably raises rates and bundles overpriced services. Drawing parallels to Apple's App Store practices and his own experiences with HEY and book sales, he contends that competition cannot function when one player dominates 90% of a market. He concludes that regulatory intervention—utility-like standards and forced spinoffs—is the only viable check on monopoly power.
key_points: Amazon's all-in fees for independent retailers rose from 19% to 30% in five years by tying storage, shipping, and advertising to its platform
Two-thirds of Amazon marketplace sales come from sellers who started in the last three years, because the squeeze churns through businesses faster than they can sustain themselves
The monopoly tollbooth dynamic is identical across Amazon, Apple, and Google—once businesses depend on you, raising rates is just turning a knob
Apple internally explored expanding its 30% cut to physical-goods apps and even considered raising rates to 40%, demonstrating the irresistible pull of monopoly extraction
DHH advocates for public utility-style regulation and mandatory spinoffs of dominant platform divisions
money_quotes: Once you capture a tollbooth like this, and you know the vast majority of those who pass through simply have to pass through, you won't be able to resist hiking rates and expanding the number of businesses who must submit.
There's no easier money made once you have a monopoly tollbooth than to grab a bigger cut for more goods and services from more dependent businesses.
It has none of the complexity or expense involved with speculative innovation, new products, making things. It's simply a knob to turn.

Competition is supposed to keep such wild increases in check! If you don't like it here, you get to go over there. But that basic market mechanism breaks down once someone like Amazon accrues this dominant level of market power.
Gmail's dominance had lead to one of the most important pieces of the internet to be suspended from innovation. Why bother?
If they squeeze hard enough, and they do, eventually there's nothing left by resentment.
Two-thirds of sales on the Amazon marketplace come from sellers who've started in the last three years, because it's difficult to make a long-term business happen on these terms.
key_insight: Big tech monopolies follow an identical, inevitable playbook: once they control a tollbooth businesses must pass through, they relentlessly raise rates and bundle overpriced services, and only regulatory intervention can break the cycle.
